romania.title: BRAȘOV: Între 23 și 26 iulie 2026, publicul este invitat în Piața Sfântul Ioan și la Bastionul Țesătorilor pentru concerte de fado și percuție.
romania.summary: După o săptămână încheiată în ritmuri de tango, Filarmonica Brașov continuă seria evenimentelor estivale cu patru seri muzicale în aer liber. Între 23 și 26 iulie, publicul este invitat în… Articolul BRAȘOV: Între 23 și 26 iulie 2026, publicul este invitat în Piața Sfântul Ioan și la Bastionul Țesătorilor pentru concerte de fado și percuție. apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Sindicaliștii din sistemul de pensii critică noua lege a salarizării: „Aceeași muncă, salarii diferite”
romania.summary: Federația Națională Solidaritatea Socială a Muncii (FNSSM) avertizează că proiectul noii Legi a salarizării personalului plătit din fonduri publice nu elimină diferențele salariale dintre angajații Casei Naționale de Pensii Publice (CNPP) și cei ai caselor teritoriale de pensii.

romania.title: Russell Crowe vine pentru prima dată în România. Fanii îl pot întâlni personal la concertele din Timișoara și București   printr-o experiență Meet & Greet exclusivă
romania.summary: După o carieră impresionantă încununată cu Premiul Oscar și roluri care au intrat în istoria cinematografiei, RUSSELL CROWE ajunge pentru prima dată în România. Nu doar în calitate de actor,… Articolul Russell Crowe vine pentru prima dată în România. Fanii îl pot întâlni personal la concertele din Timișoara și București printr-o experiență Meet & Greet exclusivă apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Ultimul eveniment de creație live „Re:Imagine Brașov – micro/MACRO” din acest an reunește șase artiști contemporani
romania.summary: Eveniment: „Re:Imagine Brașov – micro/MACRO, Ediția 2/2026” Organizator: Muzeul de Artă Brașov Artiști: Marina Aristotel, Andreea Bodea, Roxana Coatu, Magdalena Iulia Moscu, Ștefan Doru Moscu, Maria Zurbagiu Muzică: Dex, ADN… Articolul Ultimul eveniment de creație live „Re:Imagine Brașov – micro/MACRO” din acest an reunește șase artiști contemporani apare prima dată în Romania Pozitiva .

romania.title: Federația Silva cere modificarea legii salarizării. Silvicultorii avertizează că ar putea pierde 25% din salarii
romania.summary: Federația Silva solicită modificarea proiectului noii legi a salarizării personalului plătit din fonduri publice, avertizând că, dacă actul normativ va fi adoptat în forma actuală, veniturile silvicultorilor ar putea scădea cu până la 25%. Sindicaliștii susțin că proiectul elimină o prevedere din Codul silvic care include sporul de risc în salariul de bază și cer păstrarea acesteia în forma actuală.

And what he wants, according to reports, is to insert himself into photographs, regardless of whether he has any business being in them, or whether they were […] The post Persistent as Ever: Trump Somehow Manages to Photobomb 2007 Picture of Messi Bathing Yamal appeared first on Times New Roman .

romania.summary: O fi el senil, cretin şi incontinent, dar nimeni nu-l poate bănui pe preşedintele Americii, Donald Trump, că nu ştie ce vrea! În cazul acesta, să se bage în poze, indiferent că are sau nu ce căuta în ele – sau că sunt făcute acum sau în urmă cu două decenii. romania.title: De ce sunt italienii atât de vorbăreți: secretul ar putea fi școala
romania.summary: Spre deosebire de cei din alte țării, elevii italieni sunt supuși unor probe orale riguroase pe tot parcursul studiilor. Specialiștii spun că acest lucru le-a cultivat încrederea în a vorbi în public, scrie The Times.

romania.title: 😂 Dacia se laudă că în portbagajul de la modelul Striker încape o Dacia Spring
romania.summary: Producătorul auto Dacia a anunțat cu mândrie că în portbagajul noului model Striker încape integral o Dacia Spring. Reprezentanții companiei mamă au prezentat această caracteristică tehnică drept o premieră absolută pe piața auto românească și europeană, subliniind ingeniozitatea designului și versatilitatea vehiculului. Potrivit inginerilor Dacia, configurația spațială a portbagajului Striker permite transportul unei Spring întregi, […] The post Dacia se laudă că în portbagajul de la modelul Striker încape o Dacia Spring appeared first on Times New Roman .

medical.title: Crystal-clear images reveal how pregnancy antibodies cause harm in babies
medical.summary: Using advanced electron microscopy, researchers in Leiden have captured detailed images showing how, in the rare pregnancy disorder FNAIT, antibodies produced by the mother can cause bleeding in fetuses. The findings, published in Science Advances, could help pave the way for a test that identifies, during pregnancy, which babies are at greatest risk.

medical.title: Children's cereals promise breakfast fun and nutrition, but are still high in sugar
medical.summary: Many of us have grown up hearing the mantra that breakfast is the most important meal of the day, yet children's cereals fall short of that standard. That is the takeaway of new research analyzing child-targeted cereals in Canada.

medical.title: Pandemic treaty talks roll on into September
medical.summary: Countries failed to find a breakthrough after a seventh round of talks aimed at finalizing the missing piece of a pandemic treaty, the World Health Organization said Monday.

medical.title: The underground fridge that keeps food cool – even in a heatwave
medical.summary: Inspired by olden days ice houses, this underground fridge uses the earth’s stable temperature to keep food cool The post The underground fridge that keeps food cool – even in a heatwave appeared first on Positive News .

medical.title: No link between acetaminophen use during pregnancy and adverse birth outcomes, study finds
medical.summary: There is no statistically significant evidence that taking acetaminophen, also known as Tylenol, during pregnancy negatively affects how early a child is born or a child's birth weight, according to new research led by environmental scientists at the UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health.

medical.title: New research identifies challenges to human rights for people with disabilities during lockdown
medical.summary: Australia's health care system aims to protect society's most vulnerable and hidden people, including those with disabilities.

medical.title: Early intervention by a rapid response team eases patient mental state deterioration
medical.summary: The strange noises, the bright lights, the unfamiliar setting, the constant busyness—these stimuli and more can take a toll on a patient's mental well-being when they're in the hospital for a physical ailment.

medical.title: Alzheimer’s breakthrough: Scientists restore two hours of sleep without clearing brain plaques
medical.summary: Researchers have uncovered a surprising culprit behind sleep loss in Alzheimer’s disease: the brain’s own immune cells. In mice with amyloid plaques, overactive microglia triggered inflammation that kept the brain from getting enough deep, restorative sleep. Temporarily removing most of these cells restored more than two hours of sleep per day, even though the plaques remained unchanged.

medical.title: Scientists discover a protein that protects the brain from Alzheimer’s damage
medical.summary: A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. Mice with extra SORLA had less tau accumulation, less brain atrophy, and healthier connections between neurons, while mice without it experienced more severe damage. The findings also revealed a possible drug target that could help control harmful activity in supporting brain cells.

medical.title: UCLA scientists develop mineral sunscreen without the chalky white cast
medical.summary: Scientists have redesigned zinc oxide particles to create a mineral sunscreen that protects against ultraviolet radiation without leaving such a noticeable white cast. The SPF 30 formula could make sunscreen easier and more appealing to use across a wider range of skin tones.

medical.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain
medical.summary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.

medical.title: Scientists say creatine may help fight depression
medical.summary: Creatine is best known as a muscle-building supplement, but scientists are now investigating whether it could also help treat depression by boosting the brain's energy supply. A new review examined five randomized clinical trials involving 238 participants and found mixed results. Two studies, both involving women with major depressive disorder, reported that adding creatine to standard treatment improved symptoms, while three others found no meaningful benefit.

science.title: Wealthier North Carolina neighborhoods may be hotspots for insecticide-resistant tiger mosquitoes
science.summary: The choices people make to control mosquitoes in their own backyards may be helping some of those mosquitoes survive the very insecticides meant to kill them, according to two new studies from North Carolina State University. The paper on the emergence and spatial distribution of resistance appears in Parasites & Vectors. The paper on socioeconomic predictors of resistance appears in Royal Society Open Science.

science.title: Why you let down your guard on ads when scrolling on social media
science.summary: My wife stared at me in shock as I ripped open the package. The FedEx driver hadn't even made it back to his truck as I held the item up overhead like a trophy. Fumbling for words, she asked: "Why on earth did you buy a Speedo … with dinosaur tear-away trunks?"

science.title: Some dinosaurs could rise like giants until they grew too big
science.summary: Two elephant-sized South American sauropods were unusually well built for standing on their hind legs. Digital tests showed that their robust femurs handled the enormous forces better than those of larger species, especially while the dinosaurs were young. The upright pose may have helped them reach treetops, frighten predators, or impress potential mates.

science.title: Where do the world's biggest diamonds come from? Geology now has answers
science.summary: Some of the most extraordinary gems ever found belong to a rare class of diamonds known as CLIPPIRs (Cullinan-like, Large, Inclusion-Poor, Pure, Irregular, Resorbed). They make up less than 1% of all diamonds on Earth and include three of the largest diamonds ever recovered: the Cullinan, at 3,106 carats (1.4 pounds), found at the Premier mine in South Africa; the 1,111-carat Lesedi La Rona, discovered at the Karowe mine in Botswana in 2015 and sold for $53 million in 2017; and the 2,492-carat Motswedi (1.1 pounds), recovered from the Karowe mine in 2024.

science.title: How the most widespread parasite on Earth reads its genome
science.summary: A parasite carried by billions of people worldwide often causes harmful infections during pregnancy and in immunocompromised individuals and is a leading infectious cause of blindness in South America. Once it enters the body, it can rapidly multiply, spreading from one cell to the next.

science.title: Weather and human activity impact lake extremophiles
science.summary: The Great Salt Lake is separated by a 20-mile (32-kilometer) causeway into a super-salty north arm and a less-salty south arm, with each hosting distinct communities of extremophiles. These organisms mingle at a gap in the causeway, designed to regulate salinity, that has provided microbiologists with an unusual and rich opportunity to study how human activities and weather disturbances affect microbial communities in extreme environments.

science.title: Why AI's environmental footprint won't disappear into orbit
science.summary: AI companies increasingly want to move their operations into space. Elon Musk's SpaceX has applied to launch over a million AI data center satellites and ultimately build them on the moon.

science.title: Fourth-year vet students reveal clues to horse MEED and dog bone cancer
science.summary: Texas Tech University's School of Veterinary Medicine fourth-year students Kayden Tanner and Marshall Mays have both been published in international journals after putting hundreds of hours into their research papers.

science.title: Researchers advance tech that could help scientists detect habitable worlds beyond our solar system
science.summary: Are we alone in the universe? For scientists working on NASA's proposed Habitable Worlds Observatory, that question is no longer purely philosophical. It is increasingly becoming an engineering problem.

science.title: Scientists found two powerful new ways to destroy forever chemicals
science.summary: Scientists are testing two promising ways to destroy PFAS, the stubborn “forever chemicals” that can accumulate in water and resist normal treatment. One method uses collapsing vapor bubbles to generate extreme heat and reactive molecules, while the other uses cold plasma and rising gas bubbles to pull PFAS to the surface and break them apart.

environment.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
environment.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

environment.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
environment.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

environment.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
environment.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

environment.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
environment.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

environment.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
environment.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

environment.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
environment.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

environment.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
environment.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

environment.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
environment.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

The paper on the emergence and spatial distribution of resistance appears in Parasites & Vectors. The paper on socioeconomic predictors of resistance appears in Royal Society Open Science.

mix_items.title: Children's cereals promise breakfast fun and nutrition, but are still high in sugar
mix_items.summary: Many of us have grown up hearing the mantra that breakfast is the most important meal of the day, yet children's cereals fall short of that standard. That is the takeaway of new research analyzing child-targeted cereals in Canada.

mix_items.title: Why you let down your guard on ads when scrolling on social media
mix_items.summary: My wife stared at me in shock as I ripped open the package. The FedEx driver hadn't even made it back to his truck as I held the item up overhead like a trophy. Fumbling for words, she asked: "Why on earth did you buy a Speedo … with dinosaur tear-away trunks?"

mix_items.title: Pandemic treaty talks roll on into September
mix_items.summary: Countries failed to find a breakthrough after a seventh round of talks aimed at finalizing the missing piece of a pandemic treaty, the World Health Organization said Monday.

mix_items.title: The underground fridge that keeps food cool – even in a heatwave
mix_items.summary: Inspired by olden days ice houses, this underground fridge uses the earth’s stable temperature to keep food cool The post The underground fridge that keeps food cool – even in a heatwave appeared first on Positive News .

mix_items.title: Some dinosaurs could rise like giants until they grew too big
mix_items.summary: Two elephant-sized South American sauropods were unusually well built for standing on their hind legs. Digital tests showed that their robust femurs handled the enormous forces better than those of larger species, especially while the dinosaurs were young. The upright pose may have helped them reach treetops, frighten predators, or impress potential mates.

mix_items.title: Where do the world's biggest diamonds come from? Geology now has answers
mix_items.summary: Some of the most extraordinary gems ever found belong to a rare class of diamonds known as CLIPPIRs (Cullinan-like, Large, Inclusion-Poor, Pure, Irregular, Resorbed). They make up less than 1% of all diamonds on Earth and include three of the largest diamonds ever recovered: the Cullinan, at 3,106 carats (1.4 pounds), found at the Premier mine in South Africa; the 1,111-carat Lesedi La Rona, discovered at the Karowe mine in Botswana in 2015 and sold for $53 million in 2017; and the 2,492-carat Motswedi (1.1 pounds), recovered from the Karowe mine in 2024.

mix_items.title: How the most widespread parasite on Earth reads its genome
mix_items.summary: A parasite carried by billions of people worldwide often causes harmful infections during pregnancy and in immunocompromised individuals and is a leading infectious cause of blindness in South America. Once it enters the body, it can rapidly multiply, spreading from one cell to the next.

mix_items.title: Weather and human activity impact lake extremophiles
mix_items.summary: The Great Salt Lake is separated by a 20-mile (32-kilometer) causeway into a super-salty north arm and a less-salty south arm, with each hosting distinct communities of extremophiles. These organisms mingle at a gap in the causeway, designed to regulate salinity, that has provided microbiologists with an unusual and rich opportunity to study how human activities and weather disturbances affect microbial communities in extreme environments.

mix_items.title: No link between acetaminophen use during pregnancy and adverse birth outcomes, study finds
mix_items.summary: There is no statistically significant evidence that taking acetaminophen, also known as Tylenol, during pregnancy negatively affects how early a child is born or a child's birth weight, according to new research led by environmental scientists at the UNC Gillings School of Global Public Health.

mix_items.title: Why AI's environmental footprint won't disappear into orbit
mix_items.summary: AI companies increasingly want to move their operations into space. Elon Musk's SpaceX has applied to launch over a million AI data center satellites and ultimately build them on the moon.

mix_items.title: New research identifies challenges to human rights for people with disabilities during lockdown
mix_items.summary: Australia's health care system aims to protect society's most vulnerable and hidden people, including those with disabilities.

mix_items.title: Fourth-year vet students reveal clues to horse MEED and dog bone cancer
mix_items.summary: Texas Tech University's School of Veterinary Medicine fourth-year students Kayden Tanner and Marshall Mays have both been published in international journals after putting hundreds of hours into their research papers.

mix_items.title: Early intervention by a rapid response team eases patient mental state deterioration
mix_items.summary: The strange noises, the bright lights, the unfamiliar setting, the constant busyness—these stimuli and more can take a toll on a patient's mental well-being when they're in the hospital for a physical ailment.

mix_items.title: Researchers advance tech that could help scientists detect habitable worlds beyond our solar system
mix_items.summary: Are we alone in the universe? For scientists working on NASA's proposed Habitable Worlds Observatory, that question is no longer purely philosophical. It is increasingly becoming an engineering problem.

mix_items.title: Alzheimer’s breakthrough: Scientists restore two hours of sleep without clearing brain plaques
mix_items.summary: Researchers have uncovered a surprising culprit behind sleep loss in Alzheimer’s disease: the brain’s own immune cells. In mice with amyloid plaques, overactive microglia triggered inflammation that kept the brain from getting enough deep, restorative sleep. Temporarily removing most of these cells restored more than two hours of sleep per day, even though the plaques remained unchanged.

mix_items.title: Scientists discover a protein that protects the brain from Alzheimer’s damage
mix_items.summary: A protein called SORLA may act as a powerful defense against the toxic tau tangles involved in Alzheimer’s disease. Mice with extra SORLA had less tau accumulation, less brain atrophy, and healthier connections between neurons, while mice without it experienced more severe damage. The findings also revealed a possible drug target that could help control harmful activity in supporting brain cells.

mix_items.title: UCLA scientists develop mineral sunscreen without the chalky white cast
mix_items.summary: Scientists have redesigned zinc oxide particles to create a mineral sunscreen that protects against ultraviolet radiation without leaving such a noticeable white cast. The SPF 30 formula could make sunscreen easier and more appealing to use across a wider range of skin tones.

mix_items.title: Scientists found two powerful new ways to destroy forever chemicals
mix_items.summary: Scientists are testing two promising ways to destroy PFAS, the stubborn “forever chemicals” that can accumulate in water and resist normal treatment. One method uses collapsing vapor bubbles to generate extreme heat and reactive molecules, while the other uses cold plasma and rising gas bubbles to pull PFAS to the surface and break them apart.

mix_items.title: Bornean ferret badger only lives in Borneo. Could it be a conservation symbol?
mix_items.summary: The Bornean ferret badger is a small carnivore with the slinky body of a ferret and a face mask like a badger. A new study confirms that it lives only in the mountains of Sabah, a Malaysian state on the island of Borneo. Ferret badgers are nocturnal carnivores, widespread across Southeast Asia, but the Bornean […]

mix_items.title: Mozambique completes first white rhino breeding population in decades
mix_items.summary: On June 6, nine female white rhinos arrived in Mozambique’s Zinave National Park following a two-day translocation. Their arrival marks the culmination of nearly 10 years of rhino reintroduction efforts in the park, aimed at rebuilding a viable breeding population of the mammals in Zinave after decades of local extinction. The white rhinos (Ceratotherium simum) […]

mix_items.title: ‘Flamingo Revolution’ aims to stop Kushner-backed resort on protected Albanian delta
mix_items.summary: VJOSA-NARTA, Albania — In late April, heavy machinery began moving into the Pishë Poro-Narta protected landscape on Albania’s Adriatic coast without permits or public notice. Bulldozers and excavators felled coastal pine trees, flattened sand dunes, and cut new roads through previously untouched habitat. Then, barbed wire fences went up along the shoreline. The incursion was […]

mix_items.title: Scientists may have finally found how Alzheimer's spreads through the brain
mix_items.summary: A common brain protein may be giving Alzheimer’s disease an unexpected way to spread, carrying toxic Tau proteins from damaged neurons into healthy ones. By blocking these harmful protein packages before they reach new cells, researchers believe it may one day be possible to slow the disease's relentless progression.

mix_items.title: Scientists say creatine may help fight depression
mix_items.summary: Creatine is best known as a muscle-building supplement, but scientists are now investigating whether it could also help treat depression by boosting the brain's energy supply. A new review examined five randomized clinical trials involving 238 participants and found mixed results. Two studies, both involving women with major depressive disorder, reported that adding creatine to standard treatment improved symptoms, while three others found no meaningful benefit.

mix_items.title: The secret underground system keeping the Grand Canyon alive
mix_items.summary: Scientists are venturing into the Grand Canyon’s hidden cave networks to solve a mystery: how snowmelt travels underground to supply the park’s vital springs. Their discoveries could help protect the canyon’s water from drought, contamination, and other growing threats.

mix_items.title: DNA solves 250-year-old mystery of the Seychelles’ lost crocodiles
mix_items.summary: Scientists have solved the mystery of the Seychelles’ vanished crocodiles using DNA from historic museum specimens. The reptiles were not a unique species after all, but an isolated population of saltwater crocodiles that likely drifted thousands of kilometers across the Indian Ocean.

mix_items.title: Scottish wrens may be evolving into new species through island gigantism
mix_items.summary: Tiny birds on remote Scottish islands are undergoing a dramatic evolutionary transformation. Scientists studying four isolated populations of British Wrens discovered that some island birds have grown astonishingly large — with the biggest St Kilda Wrens weighing more than twice as much as the smallest mainland birds. The research suggests these wrens are evolving independently, developing unique songs, appearances, and genetics that may eventually turn them into entirely new species.

mix_items.title: A New York cemetery was hiding 5.5 million bees underground
mix_items.summary: A casual walk through an Ithaca cemetery led to the discovery of a gigantic hidden bee population — roughly 5.5 million ground-nesting bees packed beneath the soil. Scientists believe it may be one of the largest bee aggregations ever documented and say the insects are crucial pollinators for apple orchards and other crops. The bees have likely lived there for more than 100 years, thriving in the cemetery’s undisturbed sandy soil.

mix_items.title: Earth’s orbital wobble triggered rapid climate chaos during the dinosaur age
mix_items.summary: New research suggests Earth’s climate can swing wildly on surprisingly short timescales — even during hot, ice-free greenhouse periods. By studying ancient sediments from the Late Cretaceous, scientists uncovered repeating climate shifts tied to tiny changes in Earth’s orbital wobble. These cycles may have repeatedly pushed the planet between humid and arid states every few thousand years.

joke_ro.text: Mi-am pus viata in ordine. Acum nu mai gasesc nimic.

satire_ro.title: S-a aranjat pe viaţă! Hackerul care a spart Cadastrul şi-a trecut în proprietate o garsonieră la Cluj
